items.name: Inchworm to Push-Up
items.risk: The terminal push-up loads cranky shoulders/wrists and can sag the low back at the end of an unwarmed walk-out.
items.regression: Drop the push-up and just walk the hands out to a braced plank and back (per its own 'walk the hands out to a plank' cue), or sub Spider Lunge with Rotation.

items.name: A-Skip in Place
items.risk: Skipping on the balls of the feet is repetitive low-grade impact that can nag a cranky Achilles or knee.
items.regression: Do an A-march instead (drive the knee to hip height with toe up but keep one foot grounded, no hop), as the same positions without ground-strike impact.

items.name: Jumping Jacks
items.risk: Repetitive bilateral hopping is continuous impact on knees and Achilles for an athlete with accumulated wear.
items.regression: Use step-out jacks (step one foot out at a time, no jump, arms still sweeping overhead) to keep the warm-up effect with zero impact.

items.name: Butt Kicks
items.risk: The jogging ground contacts add light repetitive impact that can bother a cranky knee or Achilles.
items.regression: March in place flicking the heels toward the glutes without leaving the ground (its own 'jog in place' cue minus the hop), keeping the cadence light.

original.name: Inchworm to Push-Up
original.risk: The terminal push-up loads cranky shoulders/wrists and can sag the low back at the end of an unwarmed walk-out.
original.regression: Drop the push-up and just walk the hands out to a braced plank and back (per its own 'walk the hands out to a plank' cue), or sub Spider Lunge with Rotation.

verdict.name: Inchworm to Push-Up
verdict.rationale: Inchworm walk-out is high-value mobility for 40+, but the terminal push-up adds real (low-moderate) loaded shoulder/wrist end-range plus lumbar-sag risk on an unwarmed pattern, so scale not exclude.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Drop the push-up and walk the hands out to a braced plank and back per the exercise's own "walk the hands out to a plank" cue (bend knees as needed); if shoulders/wrists are cranky, sub Spider Lunge with Rotation, which is in the pool, same Warm-up/Mobility category, with no loaded terminal push-up.

original.name: A-Skip in Place
original.risk: Skipping on the balls of the feet is repetitive low-grade impact that can nag a cranky Achilles or knee.
original.regression: Do an A-march instead (drive the knee to hip height with toe up but keep one foot grounded, no hop), as the same positions without ground-strike impact.

verdict.name: A-Skip in Place
verdict.rationale: The "how" explicitly prescribes balls-of-feet active ground strikes, a real low-grade Achilles/calf/knee aggravator for cranky 40+ tissue; AMBER+regress keeps the movement while offering a no-hop fallback, so it is correctly calibrated, not over-restricted.
verdict.suggestedRegression: If it nags your Achilles, calf, or knee, do an A-march instead: keep one foot grounded (no hop), march in place driving the knee to hip height with the toe up and tall posture — same positions and warm-up benefit, no forefoot ground-strike impact. (A-march is the marching component named in the pool's "A-Skip + March Combo.")

original.name: Jumping Jacks
original.risk: Repetitive bilateral hopping is continuous impact on knees and Achilles for an athlete with accumulated wear.
original.regression: Use step-out jacks (step one foot out at a time, no jump, arms still sweeping overhead) to keep the warm-up effect with zero impact.

verdict.name: Jumping Jacks
verdict.rationale: Soft-landing, self-paced jumping jacks are low-amplitude impact the 40+ rules permit; keep with a plain opt-out rather than substituting the movement out by default.
verdict.suggestedRegression: If your Achilles or knees bark, do step-out jacks (step one foot out at a time, no jump, arms still sweeping overhead) to keep the warm-up effect with near-zero impact.

original.name: Butt Kicks
original.risk: The jogging ground contacts add light repetitive impact that can bother a cranky knee or Achilles.
original.regression: March in place flicking the heels toward the glutes without leaving the ground (its own 'jog in place' cue minus the hop), keeping the cadence light.

verdict.name: Butt Kicks
verdict.rationale: Impact is real but minimal (cued short ground contacts, balls of feet, low amplitude); this is a staple warm-up that should be kept GREEN with a self-scaling regression, not defaulted-down.
verdict.suggestedRegression: If your knee or Achilles is cranky, march in place flicking the heels toward the glutes without leaving the ground (same hamstring flick, no bounce), keeping the cadence light; skip the forward-traveling version.

items.name: Deep Squat Hold
items.risk: The cued below-parallel hold plus prying the knees apart loads end-range knee/hip/low-back flexion that commonly aggravates worn 40+ joints.
items.regression: If it bothers your knee, hip, or back, only sink to a comfortable depth (stop at or above parallel) per the 'comfortable depth' cue, or substitute Iso Wall Sit for a supported 90-degree hold.

original.name: Deep Squat Hold
original.risk: The cued below-parallel hold plus prying the knees apart loads end-range knee/hip/low-back flexion that commonly aggravates worn 40+ joints.
original.regression: If it bothers your knee, hip, or back, only sink to a comfortable depth (stop at or above parallel) per the 'comfortable depth' cue, or substitute Iso Wall Sit for a supported 90-degree hold.

verdict.name: Deep Squat Hold
verdict.rationale: Unloaded end-range mobility hold is high-value joint-prep for 40+, not a loaded deep squat — keep it AMBER with a depth off-ramp rather than swapping the movement out entirely.
verdict.suggestedRegression: If it bothers your knee, hip, or back, only sink to a depth that stays comfortable — stop at or above parallel and ease off prying the knees apart — or substitute the Iso Wall Sit, a wall-supported 90-degree hold that keeps your back supported and avoids end-range.

items.name: DB RDL
items.risk: Loaded hip hinge can aggravate a cranky low back if the back rounds or the range goes too deep.
items.regression: If it bothers your back, do Banded RDL instead, or per the cue shorten the range (stop at the knee, not mid-shin), slow the tempo, and drop the load.

items.name: SL DB RDL
items.risk: Single-leg loaded hinge adds balance and hip-stability demand that can strain a tweaky back or hip on a 40+ athlete.
items.regression: If balance or the back/hip complains, regress to DB RDL (two legs), or keep a fingertip on a wall and lower only to a comfortable hamstring stretch.

items.name: SL RDL (Banded)
items.risk: The single-leg balance demand can wobble a 40+ athlete and let the back round under the band's pull.
items.regression: If balance is shaky, regress to Banded RDL (two legs), or steady yourself with a fingertip on a wall and keep the range short with a flat back.

items.name: Banded Hamstring Slides
items.risk: Sliding the legs long while bridged can over-stretch and cramp aging hamstrings if the range is rushed.
items.regression: If the hamstrings cramp or strain, shorten the slide range and slow the tempo, or regress to Band Glute Bridge until tolerance builds.

original.name: DB RDL
original.risk: Loaded hip hinge can aggravate a cranky low back if the back rounds or the range goes too deep.
original.regression: If it bothers your back, do Banded RDL instead, or per the cue shorten the range (stop at the knee, not mid-shin), slow the tempo, and drop the load.

verdict.name: DB RDL
verdict.rationale: Loaded hinge genuinely loads a cranky 40+ lumbar spine, but it's a high-value technique-led pattern to keep — regress (not exclude) is correctly calibrated; the how already caps depth at mid-shin with flat back/near-vertical shins.
verdict.suggestedRegression: If it bothers your back, do Banded RDL instead (band tension is lightest at the bottom, so it de-loads the spine right where it's most vulnerable) — or per the cue shorten the range (stop at the knee, not mid-shin), slow the tempo, and drop the load.

original.name: SL DB RDL
original.risk: Single-leg loaded hinge adds balance and hip-stability demand that can strain a tweaky back or hip on a 40+ athlete.
original.regression: If balance or the back/hip complains, regress to DB RDL (two legs), or keep a fingertip on a wall and lower only to a comfortable hamstring stretch.

verdict.name: SL DB RDL
verdict.rationale: Loaded single-leg hinge genuinely stacks balance + lateral-hip + lumbar demand for a worn 40+ back/hip, so AMBER/regress (not RED, not keep) is correctly rated and not over-restricted.
verdict.suggestedRegression: If balance or the back/hip complains, regress to DB RDL (two legs) — present in the pool, same hinge pattern with the instability removed — or keep a fingertip on a wall and lower only to a comfortable hamstring stretch. (Banded RDL / SL RDL Banded also exist if even gentler bottom-range loading is wanted.)

original.name: SL RDL (Banded)
original.risk: The single-leg balance demand can wobble a 40+ athlete and let the back round under the band's pull.
original.regression: If balance is shaky, regress to Banded RDL (two legs), or steady yourself with a fingertip on a wall and keep the range short with a flat back.

verdict.name: SL RDL (Banded)
verdict.rationale: Single-leg hinge under rising band tension is a real balance-vs-spinal-position conflict for a worn 40+ back, but it's high-value unilateral work, not RED — keep it with a regression.
verdict.suggestedRegression: If balance is shaky, regress to Banded RDL (two legs), or steady yourself with a fingertip on a wall and keep the range short with a flat back.

original.name: Banded Hamstring Slides
original.risk: Sliding the legs long while bridged can over-stretch and cramp aging hamstrings if the range is rushed.
original.regression: If the hamstrings cramp or strain, shorten the slide range and slow the tempo, or regress to Band Glute Bridge until tolerance builds.

verdict.name: Banded Hamstring Slides
verdict.rationale: Long-lever eccentric hamstring load in a bridge genuinely provokes cramps/strains in aging hamstrings, but it is low-impact and valuable, so AMBER+regress is correctly calibrated.
verdict.suggestedRegression: If the hamstrings cramp or strain, shorten the slide range and slow the tempo; if it still bothers them, regress to Band Glute Bridge (short-lever, no eccentric lengthening) until tolerance builds.

items.name: SL Box Step-Down
items.risk: Slow eccentric single-leg lowering can overload a cranky knee at the bottom range and demands balance.
items.regression: Lower the box height and only descend to a comfortable depth (tap-and-hover instead of full floor tap), or substitute Box Step-Up (DBs) for a more knee-friendly single-leg stimulus.

items.name: DB Bulgarian Split Squat
items.risk: Elevated rear foot loads the front knee deeply and stresses balance, which can aggravate cranky knees in 40+ athletes.
items.regression: Reduce depth so the front thigh stops short of parallel, drop or remove the DBs, or substitute DB Goblet Lateral Lunge for a more stable loaded single-leg pattern.

original.name: SL Box Step-Down
original.risk: Slow eccentric single-leg lowering can overload a cranky knee at the bottom range and demands balance.
original.regression: Lower the box height and only descend to a comfortable depth (tap-and-hover instead of full floor tap), or substitute Box Step-Up (DBs) for a more knee-friendly single-leg stimulus.

verdict.name: SL Box Step-Down
verdict.rationale: Real PF-joint risk at the deep single-leg bottom range for cranky 40+ knees, but it's a high-value Elevate-category single-leg movement, so keep-with-regression (not exclude) is correct.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Knee bothering you at the bottom? Use a lower box and only tap-and-hover (don't sink to a full floor tap) so the standing knee never reaches its deepest, most compressed angle. Still cranky? Swap to Box Step-Up (DBs) — driving up onto a knee-height box is concentric-led and far more knee-friendly than lowering down. Keep the knee tracking over the foot and the hips level either way.

original.name: DB Bulgarian Split Squat
original.risk: Elevated rear foot loads the front knee deeply and stresses balance, which can aggravate cranky knees in 40+ athletes.
original.regression: Reduce depth so the front thigh stops short of parallel, drop or remove the DBs, or substitute DB Goblet Lateral Lunge for a more stable loaded single-leg pattern.

verdict.name: DB Bulgarian Split Squat
verdict.rationale: Rear-foot-elevated single-leg loads the front knee and demands balance — a real but manageable concern for cranky 40+ knees, so keep-with-regression (not exclude) is correct; note the "how" already stops near parallel, so the depth risk is moderate not deep.
verdict.suggestedRegression: First scale in-movement: keep the front shin near vertical, stop well short of parallel, and drop or lighten the DBs. If balance or the front knee still complains, swap to DB Goblet Lateral Lunge (both feet flat, more stable) or Box Step-Up (DBs) to a knee-height box — both are in the pool and lower-impact loaded single-leg work.

items.name: Half-Kneeling DB Press
items.risk: Full overhead lockout can pinch cranky shoulders if mobility or load is too much.
items.regression: If overhead bothers the shoulder, switch to DB Floor Press; or lighten the DB and press only to a pain-free height with ribs down.

items.name: DB Push-Up to Row
items.risk: Loaded plank-press plus heavy anti-rotation row taxes wrists and shoulders and demands trunk control.
items.regression: If wrists or shoulders complain, drop the push-up and do Renegade Row (or Band Row) for the pulling stimulus, or elevate the hands on a bench.

items.name: Deficit Push-Up
items.risk: The extended below-hand range stretches the shoulder capsule deeper than many 40+ shoulders tolerate.
items.regression: Remove the blocks and do a floor-range or hands-elevated push-up (per its own cue to keep range pain-free), or substitute Banded Chest Press.

items.name: Archer Push-Up
items.risk: Shifting nearly all bodyweight onto one arm overloads a single cranky shoulder/elbow.
items.regression: Regress to a standard Plank to Push-Up (even-loaded), or elevate the hands and keep the shift small until strength allows more.

items.name: Explosive Push-Up
items.risk: Hands leaving the floor delivers ballistic landing shock to wrists, elbows, and shoulders that aging upper-limb tendons tolerate poorly.
items.regression: Band Chest Pass delivers the same explosive press intent without any landing impact on the joints.

items.name: DB Push Press
items.risk: Explosive overhead lockout with a leg drive loads the shoulder and lumbar spine ballistically at end range.
items.regression: Use Half-Kneeling DB Press strict and lighter (removes the leg drive and limits lean), or press only to a pain-free overhead height with ribs down.

original.name: Half-Kneeling DB Press
original.risk: Full overhead lockout can pinch cranky shoulders if mobility or load is too much.
original.regression: If overhead bothers the shoulder, switch to DB Floor Press; or lighten the DB and press only to a pain-free height with ribs down.

verdict.name: Half-Kneeling DB Press
verdict.rationale: Overhead-to-lockout under load is a real aggravator for the assumed cranky 40+ shoulder, but the movement is valuable for posture/overhead health, so regress (not exclude) is correct.
verdict.suggestedRegression: If overhead bothers the shoulder, switch to DB Floor Press (in pool, no overhead component, floor caps the range); or lighten the DB and press only to a pain-free height with ribs down.

original.name: DB Push-Up to Row
original.risk: Loaded plank-press plus heavy anti-rotation row taxes wrists and shoulders and demands trunk control.
original.regression: If wrists or shoulders complain, drop the push-up and do Renegade Row (or Band Row) for the pulling stimulus, or elevate the hands on a bench.

verdict.name: DB Push-Up to Row
verdict.rationale: Loaded push-up + balancing anti-rotation row genuinely taxes worn wrists/shoulders; no impact or deep ROM, so AMBER+regress (not RED-exclude) is correct, and both named regressions exist in the pool and lower the load.
verdict.suggestedRegression: If wrists complain, do Band Row (upright, no hand-weight-bearing) for the same pulling stimulus; if shoulders are the issue, do Renegade Row (drops the push-up) or elevate the hands on a bench to ease the bottom of the press.

original.name: Deficit Push-Up
original.risk: The extended below-hand range stretches the shoulder capsule deeper than many 40+ shoulders tolerate.
original.regression: Remove the blocks and do a floor-range or hands-elevated push-up (per its own cue to keep range pain-free), or substitute Banded Chest Press.

verdict.name: Deficit Push-Up
verdict.rationale: Below-hand deficit loads the anterior shoulder capsule at end-range horizontal abduction — a deep loaded range to avoid for worn 40+ shoulders; regress the deficit, keep the valuable push pattern.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Drop the blocks and do a regular floor-range push-up (or, if shoulders complain, switch to a pool option that caps the depth: DB Floor Press — the floor limits the range — or Banded Chest Press). Note: the original cue "per its own cue to keep range pain-free" is inaccurate — the how-text gives no pain-free cue and instead instructs going below hand level.

original.name: Archer Push-Up
original.risk: Shifting nearly all bodyweight onto one arm overloads a single cranky shoulder/elbow.
original.regression: Regress to a standard Plank to Push-Up (even-loaded), or elevate the hands and keep the shift small until strength allows more.

verdict.name: Archer Push-Up
verdict.rationale: Single-arm near-full-load on a cranky shoulder/elbow is a real but controllable risk — AMBER-regress correctly scales the overload without excluding a valuable, self-limiting push pattern.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Regress to Plank to Push-Up (even-loaded, both arms share the work — present in the pool), or keep the Archer but elevate the hands and keep the side-to-side shift small until strength allows more.

original.name: Explosive Push-Up
original.risk: Hands leaving the floor delivers ballistic landing shock to wrists, elbows, and shoulders that aging upper-limb tendons tolerate poorly.
original.regression: Band Chest Pass delivers the same explosive press intent without any landing impact on the joints.

verdict.name: Explosive Push-Up
verdict.rationale: How-text confirms an airborne phase ("hands may leave the floor"), delivering ballistic closed-chain landing shock to cranky 40+ wrists/elbows/shoulders — real, correctly rated; exclude-with-substitution preserves the explosive-press value.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Band Chest Pass delivers the same explosive press intent without any landing impact on the joints.

original.name: DB Push Press
original.risk: Explosive overhead lockout with a leg drive loads the shoulder and lumbar spine ballistically at end range.
original.regression: Use Half-Kneeling DB Press strict and lighter (removes the leg drive and limits lean), or press only to a pain-free overhead height with ribs down.

verdict.name: DB Push Press
verdict.rationale: Ballistic dip-drive to overhead lockout loads cranky 40+ shoulders and lumbar spine at end range; regressing (not excluding) keeps the overhead pattern while removing the leg drive and lean.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Use Half-Kneeling DB Press strict and lighter (half-kneeling base removes the leg drive; cue "press straight overhead without leaning back, ribs down" limits lumbar extension), or press only to a pain-free overhead height. Both are in the pool and self-coachable.

items.name: Hanging / Bar Hold
items.risk: Full passive hang loads cranky shoulders and grip, and can strain an arthritic or unstable shoulder if hung dead-weight.
items.regression: Keep feet partly on the floor to offload bodyweight and shorten the hold, building time gradually as the exercise's own how-text advises; or substitute Band Row for a fully supported pulling stimulus.

items.name: Renegade Row
items.risk: The high-plank anti-rotation row loads a cranky shoulder under bodyweight while demanding wrist extension and trunk control that can strain a stiff lower back.
items.regression: Substitute Band Row for the same horizontal pull without shoulder-weight-bearing, or widen the feet and drop the load as the how-text suggests to cut the anti-rotation demand.

original.name: Hanging / Bar Hold
original.risk: Full passive hang loads cranky shoulders and grip, and can strain an arthritic or unstable shoulder if hung dead-weight.
original.regression: Keep feet partly on the floor to offload bodyweight and shorten the hold, building time gradually as the exercise's own how-text advises; or substitute Band Row for a fully supported pulling stimulus.

verdict.name: Hanging / Bar Hold
verdict.rationale: Active packed hang still taxes cranky cuff/grip in 40+ players, so AMBER-regress is right; the floor-assist scaling and the in-pool Band Row are both valid lower-risk options.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Keep feet partly on the floor to offload bodyweight and shorten the hold, building time gradually as the how-text advises; or substitute the pool's Band Row (or Band Face Pull) for a fully supported, joint-friendly pulling stimulus.

original.name: Renegade Row
original.risk: The high-plank anti-rotation row loads a cranky shoulder under bodyweight while demanding wrist extension and trunk control that can strain a stiff lower back.
original.regression: Substitute Band Row for the same horizontal pull without shoulder-weight-bearing, or widen the feet and drop the load as the how-text suggests to cut the anti-rotation demand.

verdict.name: Renegade Row
verdict.rationale: Loaded high-plank support shoulder + wrist extension + anti-rotation lumbar demand is a real concern for accumulated-wear 40+ shoulders/backs; not RED (no impact/failure/ballistics) but not a clean default GREEN, so AMBER/regress is correctly tiered.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Substitute Band Row (in pool, Upper Body / Pull) for the same horizontal pull from a standing braced stance with no shoulder weight-bearing or wrist-extension load; if the lower back is the main limiter, regress further to Band Face Pull or Dead Bug for the anti-rotation work.

items.name: Hollow Rock
items.risk: The overhead-arm lever and rocking pull the lumbar into extension once the dish breaks down, aggravating cranky backs.
items.regression: Drop the rock and hold a static hollow position with arms by the sides / knees bent to shorten the lever; if the back still complains, do Dead Bug with DB instead.

items.name: Copenhagen Plank — Long
items.risk: The long-lever adductor load places very high tension on the groin, a frequent hockey strain site that gets less forgiving with age.
items.regression: Shorten the lever by placing the bent top knee (not the ankle) on the bench for a short-Copenhagen hold; if the groin still complains, do Side Plank with Reach instead.

items.name: Dragon Flag Negatives
items.risk: Lowering a long rigid body lever is max anti-extension load with high lumbar shear and a real risk of losing control onto the back.
items.regression: Pallof March

items.name: Hanging Straight-Leg Raise
items.risk: Straight-leg hanging raises heavily load the hip flexors and lumbar while demanding a full grip/shoulder hang, taxing cranky backs and shoulders at once.
items.regression: Dead Bug with DB

items.name: Bear Crawl with DBs
items.risk: Gripping DBs in the weight-bearing hands loads cranky wrists and shoulders in a flexed, compressed position throughout the crawl.
items.regression: Drop the dumbbells and perform Bear Crawl Variations on flat palms (or fists on a mat to keep the wrists neutral).

original.name: Hollow Rock
original.risk: The overhead-arm lever and rocking pull the lumbar into extension once the dish breaks down, aggravating cranky backs.
original.regression: Drop the rock and hold a static hollow position with arms by the sides / knees bent to shorten the lever; if the back still complains, do Dead Bug with DB instead.

verdict.name: Hollow Rock
verdict.rationale: Long-lever overhead anti-extension + rocking momentum genuinely cycles cranky 40+ lumbars into extension when the dish fails; scaling (not excluding) is correctly rated AMBER.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Shorten the lever first: hold a static hollow with arms by the sides and knees bent (no rocking), keeping the low back pressed flat. If the back still complains, switch to Dead Bug (in the pool as "Dead Bug with DB") — and go bodyweight-only, no DB, if lumbar control is the limiter, focusing on keeping the back flat and ribs down.

original.name: Copenhagen Plank — Long
original.risk: The long-lever adductor load places very high tension on the groin, a frequent hockey strain site that gets less forgiving with age.
original.regression: Shorten the lever by placing the bent top knee (not the ankle) on the bench for a short-Copenhagen hold; if the groin still complains, do Side Plank with Reach instead.

verdict.name: Copenhagen Plank — Long
verdict.rationale: Long-lever Copenhagen genuinely maxes adductor tension at a top hockey-strain site; AMBER+regress is correct — valuable enough to keep but must default to a shorter lever for 40+ tissue.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Shorten the lever by placing the bent top knee (not the ankle) on the bench for a short-Copenhagen hold; if the groin still complains, do Side Plank with Reach instead.

original.name: Dragon Flag Negatives
original.risk: Lowering a long rigid body lever is max anti-extension load with high lumbar shear and a real risk of losing control onto the back.
original.regression: Pallof March

verdict.name: Dragon Flag Negatives
verdict.rationale: Max anti-extension long-lever load with high lumbar shear and a fall-onto-extended-back failure mode is correctly RED for a 40+ player with lumbar wear; but the regression should match the trained quality.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Dead Bug with DB

original.name: Hanging Straight-Leg Raise
original.risk: Straight-leg hanging raises heavily load the hip flexors and lumbar while demanding a full grip/shoulder hang, taxing cranky backs and shoulders at once.
original.regression: Dead Bug with DB

verdict.name: Hanging Straight-Leg Raise
verdict.rationale: Stacks lumbar (straight-leg hip-flexor lever pulling on the spine) and a loaded grip/shoulder dead-hang at once — two vulnerable 40+ tissues with no easy in-set scaling; correctly RED.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Dead Bug with DB

original.name: Bear Crawl with DBs
original.risk: Gripping DBs in the weight-bearing hands loads cranky wrists and shoulders in a flexed, compressed position throughout the crawl.
original.regression: Drop the dumbbells and perform Bear Crawl Variations on flat palms (or fists on a mat to keep the wrists neutral).

verdict.name: Bear Crawl with DBs
verdict.rationale: Gripped DBs add loaded/unstable wrist-shoulder stress in a weight-bearing crawl with no anti-rotation benefit beyond the bodyweight version, so regressing to the bodyweight bear crawl keeps the value and drops the cost.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Drop the dumbbells and perform "Bear Crawl Variations" (bodyweight) on flat palms — or on fists on a mat to keep the wrists neutral — keeping knees low, hips level, and resisting any twist.

items.name: Squat to Tuck Jump
items.risk: Max-amplitude double-leg jump with knee-to-chest tuck creates a high-impact landing that shocks cranky knees and the low back.
items.regression: Low Box Hop-Up — Stick

items.name: Broad Jump → Sprint 10m
items.risk: Maximal horizontal jump landed straight into an acceleration sprint is a ballistic Achilles/hamstring strain trap with no time to absorb impact.
items.regression: Skater Hop — Stick It

items.name: Lateral Skater Bound
items.risk: Continuous reactive bounding with minimal ground contact loads knees and Achilles repeatedly without a reset between reps.
items.regression: Regress to Skater Hop — Stick It: bound, stick and balance each landing rather than rebounding continuously, keeping amplitude moderate and volume low.

items.name: Skater Hop Complex
items.risk: The continuous reactive bounds within the sequence stress knees and Achilles with little recovery between contacts.
items.regression: Drop the continuous-bound portion and perform only the maximal stuck-landing bounds (as in Skater Hop — Stick It), holding each landing balanced before resetting.

items.name: Multi-Direction Bound
items.risk: Explosive bounding into varied/diagonal directions adds unpredictable rotational shear to knees and ankles on landing.
items.regression: Land softly on BOTH legs and fully stick/control each landing before the next bound, reducing amplitude and skipping the single-leg diagonal landings if a knee or ankle complains.

items.name: Single-Leg Pogo Hops
items.risk: Continuous stiff-knee single-leg rebounding concentrates repeated impact on one Achilles and calf, a common 40+ tendon injury site.
items.regression: Regress to Ankle Hop Stick on two feet, or do single hops that you stick and reset between rather than continuous rebounds; keep amplitude small and reps low.

items.name: High-Low Pogo Combo
items.risk: The higher-amplitude pogo bouts raise impact and Achilles load as fatigue accumulates over continuous contacts.
items.regression: Drop the high-amplitude portion and keep only the low, fast, small-amplitude hops (as in Ankle Hop Stick), staying tall on the balls of the feet with short quiet contacts.

items.name: Depth Drop (Low Box)
items.risk: Even from a low box, the step-off landing delivers a shock load that can aggravate cranky knees if the box is too tall or the landing collapses.
items.regression: Use the lowest possible box (or a curb) and stick the landing softly; if a knee complains, replace with Drop Squat, which trains the same absorb-and-stick pattern without the drop.

items.name: Banded Squat Jump
items.risk: Explosive vertical jumping adds a repeated landing impact to the knees and low back despite the band assisting deceleration on the way up.
items.regression: Stay in a quarter-squat depth, jump at moderate (not maximal) amplitude, and land softly with bent knees; or substitute Low Box Hop-Up — Stick to remove the hard floor landing.

items.name: Contrast Jump
items.risk: Pairing a loaded effort with maximal-intent unloaded jumps drives peak-output landings that overload tendons and joints beyond safe tissue tolerance for 40+.
items.regression: Banded Squat Jump

items.name: Squat to Broad Jump
items.risk: A pause-then-explode maximal broad jump for distance produces a high horizontal landing impact that strains knees, hamstrings and Achilles.
items.regression: Skater Hop — Stick It

items.name: Continuous Lateral Bound
items.risk: The continuous chain minimizes ground-contact time, giving knees and Achilles no chance to reset between repeated reactive landings.
items.regression: Convert to stuck-landing skater hops (Skater Hop — Stick It): push off, land on the outside leg, and hold balanced before the next bound rather than rebounding continuously.

items.name: Crossover Step Bound
items.risk: The lateral crossover bound adds a twisting load across a planted knee on each landing, risky for cranky knees.
items.regression: Slow the tempo to a controlled crossover STEP-and-stick rather than an explosive bound, or replace with Skater Hop — Stick It for the same lateral push without the rotational landing.

items.regression: Skater Hop — Stick It

items.regression: Regress to a two-leg jump with stuck landing (Low Box Hop-Up — Stick), or perform the single-leg version at moderate amplitude with a soft, well-controlled stick and low volume.

original.name: Squat to Tuck Jump
original.risk: Max-amplitude double-leg jump with knee-to-chest tuck creates a high-impact landing that shocks cranky knees and the low back.
original.regression: Low Box Hop-Up — Stick

verdict.name: Squat to Tuck Jump
verdict.rationale: Max-amplitude double-leg tuck jump = high ground-reaction-force landing with no soft-landing variant; the tuck itself is the hazard, so RED/exclude is correct for cranky 40+ knees/back/Achilles.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Low Box Hop-Up — Stick

original.name: Broad Jump → Sprint 10m
original.risk: Maximal horizontal jump landed straight into an acceleration sprint is a ballistic Achilles/hamstring strain trap with no time to absorb impact.
original.regression: Skater Hop — Stick It

verdict.name: Broad Jump → Sprint 10m
verdict.rationale: Maximal horizontal jump with a deliberately UNABSORBED landing converted straight into an accelerating sprint is a textbook Achilles/hamstring ballistic trap that violates the 40+ no-max-impact / moderate-amplitude rules; not over-restricted since safer reactive options retain the value.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Skater Hop — Stick It

original.name: Lateral Skater Bound
original.risk: Continuous reactive bounding with minimal ground contact loads knees and Achilles repeatedly without a reset between reps.
original.regression: Regress to Skater Hop — Stick It: bound, stick and balance each landing rather than rebounding continuously, keeping amplitude moderate and volume low.

verdict.name: Lateral Skater Bound
verdict.rationale: Continuous reactive bounding with minimized ground contact is real tendon/joint shock for a worn 40+ athlete, but the valuable lateral skater pattern survives intact in the stick-it variant, so regress (not exclude) is correct.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Regress to Skater Hop — Stick It (present in pool, same movement family): bound, stick and balance each landing rather than rebounding continuously, absorbing each landing quietly with a bent knee and hips back; keep amplitude moderate and volume low. If knees or Achilles still complain, shorten the push-off distance further or drop to lateral step-overs.

original.name: Skater Hop Complex
original.risk: The continuous reactive bounds within the sequence stress knees and Achilles with little recovery between contacts.
original.regression: Drop the continuous-bound portion and perform only the maximal stuck-landing bounds (as in Skater Hop — Stick It), holding each landing balanced before resetting.

verdict.name: Skater Hop Complex
verdict.rationale: Continuous reactive bounds are genuine fast-SSC Achilles/patellar shock for a worn 40+ athlete; regressing to the existing stuck-landing version keeps the lateral power/balance value while removing the shock.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Drop the continuous-bound portion and perform only the maximal stuck-landing bounds (as in Skater Hop — Stick It), holding each landing balanced before resetting.

original.name: Multi-Direction Bound
original.risk: Explosive bounding into varied/diagonal directions adds unpredictable rotational shear to knees and ankles on landing.
original.regression: Land softly on BOTH legs and fully stick/control each landing before the next bound, reducing amplitude and skipping the single-leg diagonal landings if a knee or ankle complains.

verdict.name: Multi-Direction Bound
verdict.rationale: Real risk: diagonal/varied-vector bounding plus the single-leg landing option adds rotational shear to wear-prone masters knees/ankles, so AMBER+regress (not exclude, not GREEN) is correctly calibrated for a still-competitive 40+ player.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Land softly on BOTH legs and fully stick/control each landing before the next bound, with reduced amplitude — skip the single-leg diagonal landings if a knee or ankle complains. The pool already permits "one or both legs" for this movement, so this is a valid in-exercise scaling. If two-foot landings still aggravate the joint, drop to a no-flight bilateral option from the pool — "Drop Squat" (snap into a stuck athletic landing) or "Ankle Hop Stick" for the lowest-impact reactive version.

original.name: Single-Leg Pogo Hops
original.risk: Continuous stiff-knee single-leg rebounding concentrates repeated impact on one Achilles and calf, a common 40+ tendon injury site.
original.regression: Regress to Ankle Hop Stick on two feet, or do single hops that you stick and reset between rather than continuous rebounds; keep amplitude small and reps low.

verdict.name: Single-Leg Pogo Hops
verdict.rationale: "How" text confirms continuous stiff-knee single-leg rebounding loads one Achilles/calf in fast repeated SSC with no recovery — a real 40+ tendon risk; valuable movement, so scale not exclude.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Regress to "Ankle Hop Stick" (in pool): same stiff-ankle hop but on two feet, sticking and resetting each landing quietly under control instead of continuous rebounding. Or do single-leg hops you stick and reset between reps — keep amplitude small, reps low, and skip if it bothers your Achilles or calf.

original.name: High-Low Pogo Combo
original.risk: The higher-amplitude pogo bouts raise impact and Achilles load as fatigue accumulates over continuous contacts.
original.regression: Drop the high-amplitude portion and keep only the low, fast, small-amplitude hops (as in Ankle Hop Stick), staying tall on the balls of the feet with short quiet contacts.

verdict.name: High-Low Pogo Combo
verdict.rationale: High-amplitude, continuous reactive pogos load the Achilles/calf as fatigue accumulates — a textbook masters-athlete risk; regress (not exclude) preserves the valuable low-amplitude ankle stimulus.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Do Ankle Hop Stick instead: from a tall stance, perform a single low, stiff-ankle hop, land quietly on the balls of the feet, and stick/hold the landing under control before resetting. Skip the high-amplitude hops entirely; keep amplitude small, contacts soft, and volume low. If your Achilles or calf is cranky, drop it altogether and warm the ankles with calf raises.

original.name: Depth Drop (Low Box)
original.risk: Even from a low box, the step-off landing delivers a shock load that can aggravate cranky knees if the box is too tall or the landing collapses.
original.regression: Use the lowest possible box (or a curb) and stick the landing softly; if a knee complains, replace with Drop Squat, which trains the same absorb-and-stick pattern without the drop.

verdict.name: Depth Drop (Low Box)
verdict.rationale: Low-box step-off + bilateral stick is a protective landing drill, not max-impact plyo; keep at AMBER with the Drop Squat swap offered rather than mandated.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Use the lowest possible box (or a curb) and stick the landing softly and quietly; if a knee complains, replace with Drop Squat (confirmed in pool) — it trains the same absorb-and-stick pattern by dropping your own bodyweight into the catch, with no fall through space and therefore lower landing shock.

original.name: Banded Squat Jump
original.risk: Explosive vertical jumping adds a repeated landing impact to the knees and low back despite the band assisting deceleration on the way up.
original.regression: Stay in a quarter-squat depth, jump at moderate (not maximal) amplitude, and land softly with bent knees; or substitute Low Box Hop-Up — Stick to remove the hard floor landing.

verdict.name: Banded Squat Jump
verdict.rationale: Risk is real and correctly rated: the "how" cues maximal-intent repeated jumps with a hard floor landing the band does not soften — exactly the max-impact plyo the 40+ rules flag; AMBER/regress (not exclude) rightly keeps the valuable power stimulus while scaling the impact.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Drop the "maximal intent" cue: stay in a quarter-squat, jump at moderate (sub-maximal) amplitude, land soft with bent knees, and keep volume low. If knees, Achilles, or low back complain, substitute "Low Box Hop-Up — Stick" (jump up onto a low box, step back down) to remove the hard floor landing entirely.

original.name: Contrast Jump
original.risk: Pairing a loaded effort with maximal-intent unloaded jumps drives peak-output landings that overload tendons and joints beyond safe tissue tolerance for 40+.
original.regression: Banded Squat Jump

verdict.name: Contrast Jump
verdict.rationale: Maximal-intent peak-output landings are the inseparable core of the movement (PAP contrast method) — real, correctly-rated tendon/joint overload risk for 40+; cannot be softened without changing the exercise.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Low Box Hop-Up — Stick

original.name: Squat to Broad Jump
original.risk: A pause-then-explode maximal broad jump for distance produces a high horizontal landing impact that strains knees, hamstrings and Achilles.
original.regression: Skater Hop — Stick It

verdict.name: Squat to Broad Jump
verdict.rationale: Max-distance broad jump = high horizontal landing/braking force on Achilles, hamstrings and patellar tendon — exactly the ballistic shock the 40+ rules prohibit; RED/exclude is correct, not over-restricted.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Skater Hop — Stick It (present in pool; self-governed sub-maximal lateral bound with explicit soft/stick landing, lower impact and more hockey-specific — cue "moderate hop distance, no max efforts").

original.name: Continuous Lateral Bound
original.risk: The continuous chain minimizes ground-contact time, giving knees and Achilles no chance to reset between repeated reactive landings.
original.regression: Convert to stuck-landing skater hops (Skater Hop — Stick It): push off, land on the outside leg, and hold balanced before the next bound rather than rebounding continuously.

verdict.name: Continuous Lateral Bound
verdict.rationale: Continuous reactive chain with explicit "minimize ground-contact time" cyclically shocks Achilles/patellar tendon — real risk for worn 40+ tissue; regress (not exclude) since the lateral pattern is valuable.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Convert to stuck-landing skater hops (Skater Hop — Stick It): push off, land softly on the outside leg, and hold balanced before the next bound rather than rebounding continuously.

original.name: Crossover Step Bound
original.risk: The lateral crossover bound adds a twisting load across a planted knee on each landing, risky for cranky knees.
original.regression: Slow the tempo to a controlled crossover STEP-and-stick rather than an explosive bound, or replace with Skater Hop — Stick It for the same lateral push without the rotational landing.

verdict.name: Crossover Step Bound
verdict.rationale: Continuous crossover bound keeps hips square while the trailing leg crosses in front, loading a transverse-plane twist on a planted knee during reactive decel/reaccel — real for 40+ knees, but valuable enough to scale rather than cut.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Slow the tempo to a controlled crossover STEP-and-stick rather than an explosive bound, or replace with Skater Hop — Stick It (in pool): a pure frontal-plane lateral push that "sticks" each landing, removing the rotational/reactive shock while keeping the same lateral push training effect.

original.name: Lateral Depth Drop to Bound
original.risk: A step-off drop pre-loading an immediate reactive single-leg lateral bound stacks shock impact plus rotational shear on a vulnerable knee/Achilles.
original.regression: Skater Hop — Stick It

verdict.name: Lateral Depth Drop to Bound
verdict.rationale: Box step-off adds free-fall shock onto a single-leg reactive lateral bound with a minimize-ground-time cue — stacked ballistic impact plus frontal-plane shear on knee/Achilles, squarely in the max-impact-plyo prohibition for 40+.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Skater Hop — Stick It

original.name: SL CMJ Stick
original.risk: A maximal single-leg vertical jump concentrates the takeoff and landing impact on one knee and Achilles.
original.regression: Regress to a two-leg jump with stuck landing (Low Box Hop-Up — Stick), or perform the single-leg version at moderate amplitude with a soft, well-controlled stick and low volume.

verdict.name: SL CMJ Stick
verdict.rationale: Maximal single-leg jump-and-stick concentrates ballistic takeoff/landing shock on one knee/Achilles — correctly AMBER+regress for cranky 40+ tissue, but valuable enough not to exclude.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Regress to a two-leg jump with stuck landing (Low Box Hop-Up — Stick, confirmed in pool — two-leg takeoff and box-height landing cut impact), or perform the single-leg version at moderate amplitude with a soft, well-controlled stick and low volume to keep the skating-specific single-leg transfer.

original.name: Hurdle Hops — Continuous
original.risk: Continuous stiff reactive double-leg hops over a hurdle row are high-amplitude shock work with a trip/fall hazard, hard on knees and Achilles.
original.regression: Ankle Hop Stick

verdict.name: Hurdle Hops — Continuous
verdict.rationale: Continuous stiff-reactive double-leg hopping over a hurdle row is sustained max-impact shock to Achilles/patellar tendon plus a real trip/fall hazard — squarely the masters "no ballistic shock / no max-impact plyo" exclusion; the defining quality can't be kept while removing the risk.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Ankle Hop Stick

items.name: Reactive 5m Sprint
items.risk: Reacting and accelerating hard from a dead stop loads cold calf/hamstring tissue that is strain-prone in 40+ athletes.
items.regression: Build in first with Hill Stride (submaximal, controlled mechanics) and keep effort to ~80% on a thoroughly warm sprint before adding reactive cues.

items.name: Reactive Mirror Sprint
items.risk: Unpredictable open-skill direction changes invite awkward planting and knee/groin tweaks in worn 40+ joints.
items.regression: Slow the partner to walk/jog speed and pre-set the directions (closed-skill) so changes are anticipated, like Crossover Step practiced both ways.

items.name: Resisted → Free Sprint
items.risk: The sudden release into max free sprinting spikes hamstring eccentric load at top speed, a classic 40+ pull mechanism.
items.regression: Keep the free portion submaximal and use light band tension, or substitute Hill Stride for the same overload-driving stimulus without the release spike.

items.name: Mirror Sprint Game
items.risk: Competitive escape/chase with unpredictable cuts maximizes fall and groin/knee strain risk for aging tissue.
items.regression: Cap effort and play it as a slower shuffle-only mirror, or substitute Reactive Lateral Start with pre-set directions for controlled reactivity.

items.name: A-Skip + B-Skip
items.risk: The B-skip's active lower-leg extension and 'paw-back' loads the hamstring near full length, where 40+ athletes most often strain.
items.regression: Drop the B portion and stay with A-Skip Moving until well warm, adding only a gentle, partial-range leg extension on the B.

items.name: Reactive Lateral Start
items.risk: Explosive low lateral first-steps with a drop step stress the groin and inside-of-knee structures common trouble spots at 40+.
items.regression: Make it a planned (non-reactive) crossover at moderate effort, as in Crossover Step, before layering in the reactive cue.

items.name: 30m Fly Sprint
items.risk: Holding true maximal top-end velocity for 30m is the highest hamstring-strain demand and tough on Achilles for 40+ legs.
items.regression: Shorten to a Flying 10m Sprint or run the zone at ~90% relaxed effort with full recovery and thorough warm-up first.

items.name: Reactive 4-Cone
items.risk: Sharp reactive cuts and plant-and-redirect at speed load knees and groin under unpredictable angles, raising strain/fall risk.
items.regression: Run it as a pre-planned (non-reactive) cone pattern at controlled speed, or use Reactive 5m Sprint regressed to linear-only first.

items.name: Flying 10m Sprint
items.risk: A short max-velocity zone still loads hamstrings and Achilles near their limit, where 40+ athletes pull.
items.regression: Cap the fly zone at ~90% relaxed effort with a long build-in and full recovery, or substitute Hill Stride for safer high-quality mechanics work.

items.name: Crossover Step to Sprint
items.risk: Converting a lateral crossover into a forward sprint combines groin-stretching plant with hard acceleration onto cold tissue.
items.regression: Keep the crossover at moderate effort and let the sprint-out stay submaximal, or drill just the Crossover Step until the pattern is grooved.

items.name: Hill Sprint
items.risk: All-out incline sprinting drives a maximal calf/Achilles and hamstring push that can shock cranky 40+ lower-leg tissue.
items.regression: Use Hill Stride (submaximal, clean mechanics) on a moderate grade instead of the all-out effort until well-conditioned.

items.name: 30-Sec Shuttle
items.risk: Repeated max sprint-decelerate-turn cycles for 30s hammer the knees and groin and cook hamstrings as fatigue sets in.
items.regression: Lengthen the shuttle distance to soften the turns, shorten the bout, and run at ~80% so decelerations stay controlled.

original.name: Reactive 5m Sprint
original.risk: Reacting and accelerating hard from a dead stop loads cold calf/hamstring tissue that is strain-prone in 40+ athletes.
original.regression: Build in first with Hill Stride (submaximal, controlled mechanics) and keep effort to ~80% on a thoroughly warm sprint before adding reactive cues.

verdict.name: Reactive 5m Sprint
verdict.rationale: "Sprint maximally" + reactive cue from a dead stop is real posterior-chain strain risk for 40+; AMBER/regress is correct, not over-restricted.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Same-movement scaling first: on thoroughly warm legs, self-initiate (no reaction cue) and cap effort at ~80% over the 5m, focusing on smooth acceleration; only after several clean reps add the reactive cue back in. If the calf/hamstring feels cranky, substitute Hill Stride (submaximal uphill, controlled mechanics) which naturally limits speed and eccentric load.

original.name: Reactive Mirror Sprint
original.risk: Unpredictable open-skill direction changes invite awkward planting and knee/groin tweaks in worn 40+ joints.
original.regression: Slow the partner to walk/jog speed and pre-set the directions (closed-skill) so changes are anticipated, like Crossover Step practiced both ways.

verdict.name: Reactive Mirror Sprint
verdict.rationale: Open-skill unanticipated maximal cutting is a real knee/groin risk in worn 40+ joints, but it is bodyweight and low-volume, so regress (not exclude) to a self-paced closed-skill version is correct.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Drop the reactive partner element: practice the closed-skill Crossover Step instead, controlling your own pace and pre-planning each direction so every plant is anticipated. Push off explosively in both directions at a speed your knees and groin tolerate; if any cut still bothers a joint, slow to a controlled shuffle.

original.name: Resisted → Free Sprint
original.risk: The sudden release into max free sprinting spikes hamstring eccentric load at top speed, a classic 40+ pull mechanism.
original.regression: Keep the free portion submaximal and use light band tension, or substitute Hill Stride for the same overload-driving stimulus without the release spike.

verdict.name: Resisted → Free Sprint
verdict.rationale: Contrast release into "light and fast" overspeed sprinting spikes late-swing hamstring eccentric load at top velocity — the classic 40+ acute pull mechanism; AMBER/regress (not exclude) is correct since the drive stimulus is preserved via scaling.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Keep the free portion submaximal with light band tension only (no max release), or substitute pool exercise "Hill Stride" — controlled, submaximal incline striding that delivers the same push-off/drive overload without the overspeed release spike. (Do NOT substitute "Hill Sprint," which is maximal.)

original.name: Mirror Sprint Game
original.risk: Competitive escape/chase with unpredictable cuts maximizes fall and groin/knee strain risk for aging tissue.
original.regression: Cap effort and play it as a slower shuffle-only mirror, or substitute Reactive Lateral Start with pre-set directions for controlled reactivity.

verdict.name: Mirror Sprint Game
verdict.rationale: Unpredictable partner-driven cuts at explicitly maximal/competitive escape effort genuinely maxes ACL/MCL and adductor-strain risk for aging tissue, but the reactive pattern is valuable and scalable, so regress (not exclude) is correctly calibrated.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Cap effort and play as a slower, shuffle-only mirror with no sprinting/escape, OR substitute Reactive Lateral Start (in pool, same category) using pre-set directions — discrete reps with full controlled deceleration and reset between, no competitive chase. If it tweaks your knee, back, or groin, drop to predictable pre-called directions at sub-maximal speed.

original.name: A-Skip + B-Skip
original.risk: The B-skip's active lower-leg extension and 'paw-back' loads the hamstring near full length, where 40+ athletes most often strain.
original.regression: Drop the B portion and stay with A-Skip Moving until well warm, adding only a gentle, partial-range leg extension on the B.

verdict.name: A-Skip + B-Skip
verdict.rationale: B-skip's active full-length lower-leg extension + paw-back is a real lengthened-eccentric hamstring load that 40+ tissue tolerates poorly cold; AMBER/regress (not exclude) correctly keeps the valuable A-skip and scales only the high-strain B component.
verdict.suggestedRegression: If the B-skip's leg-flick tugs your hamstring, drop it and do "A-Skip Moving" (knee drive, toe up, tall posture, active paw-back, no leg extension) until thoroughly warm; only then add the B as a small, partial-range lower-leg extension, never a forceful full-length whip.

original.name: Reactive Lateral Start
original.risk: Explosive low lateral first-steps with a drop step stress the groin and inside-of-knee structures common trouble spots at 40+.
original.regression: Make it a planned (non-reactive) crossover at moderate effort, as in Crossover Step, before layering in the reactive cue.

verdict.name: Reactive Lateral Start
verdict.rationale: Ground-level reactive start-and-accelerate (no box, no bound, no landing shock); genuine groin/medial-knee risk is the drop step under a surprise cue, which is manageable by cueing crossover + sub-max first reps rather than regressing away the reactive value.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Default to the crossover (skip the drop step) and start sub-maximal, increasing cue speed only as it feels clean; if it nags your groin or inside knee, drop the reactive cue and do planned Crossover Step at moderate effort in both directions before re-adding the reaction.

original.name: 30m Fly Sprint
original.risk: Holding true maximal top-end velocity for 30m is the highest hamstring-strain demand and tough on Achilles for 40+ legs.
original.regression: Shorten to a Flying 10m Sprint or run the zone at ~90% relaxed effort with full recovery and thorough warm-up first.

verdict.name: 30m Fly Sprint
verdict.rationale: Holding true max velocity over a full 30m zone (plus 10-20m build-in) is the pool's highest hamstring-strain and Achilles demand for worn 40+ legs; valuable enough to keep via regression, not exclude.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Shorten to a Flying 10m Sprint (a real pool entry; same max-velocity pattern, two-thirds less time in the strain zone) or run the zone at ~90% relaxed effort with full recovery and thorough warm-up first.

original.name: Reactive 4-Cone
original.risk: Sharp reactive cuts and plant-and-redirect at speed load knees and groin under unpredictable angles, raising strain/fall risk.
original.regression: Run it as a pre-planned (non-reactive) cone pattern at controlled speed, or use Reactive 5m Sprint regressed to linear-only first.

verdict.name: Reactive 4-Cone
verdict.rationale: Unanticipated sharp plant-and-cut at speed is a real ACL/MCL/groin mechanism for accumulated-wear 40+ knees/groin, so AMBER+regress is correct; the pre-planned controlled-speed version preserves the valuable hockey pattern.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Run it as a pre-planned (non-reactive) cone pattern at controlled, sub-maximal speed with rounded rather than sharp plants, so you know the next cone in advance. If your knee, groin, or back complains, drop to the Reactive 5m Sprint as linear-only (react then accelerate straight, no cutting).

original.name: Flying 10m Sprint
original.risk: A short max-velocity zone still loads hamstrings and Achilles near their limit, where 40+ athletes pull.
original.regression: Cap the fly zone at ~90% relaxed effort with a long build-in and full recovery, or substitute Hill Stride for safer high-quality mechanics work.

verdict.name: Flying 10m Sprint
verdict.rationale: Max-velocity fly is the #1 hamstring/Achilles pull mechanism for 40+ athletes; valuable as mechanics work, so regress (not exclude) with a capped-effort option plus a real pool substitute (Hill Stride).
verdict.suggestedRegression: Run the fly zone at ~90% relaxed, smooth effort with a long, gradual build-in and full recovery between reps — never a true all-out top gear. If your hamstring or Achilles feels tight or twingy, switch to Hill Stride: stride a moderate incline at controlled submaximal effort focusing on clean mechanics, which caps your speed and shortens the stride so the hamstring is never stretched at full velocity.

original.name: Crossover Step to Sprint
original.risk: Converting a lateral crossover into a forward sprint combines groin-stretching plant with hard acceleration onto cold tissue.
original.regression: Keep the crossover at moderate effort and let the sprint-out stay submaximal, or drill just the Crossover Step until the pattern is grooved.

verdict.name: Crossover Step to Sprint
verdict.rationale: Real but moderate groin/adductor strain risk from the open-hip crossover plant into hard acceleration; no impact shock, so AMBER+regress (not exclude) is correct for cranky 40+ tissue.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Warm the groin/hips first, then keep the crossover at moderate effort and the sprint-out submaximal. If your groin feels tight or cold, do the standalone "Crossover Step" (same lateral pattern, no sprint transition) until the movement feels smooth, then add a gentle build-up to the sprint-out.

original.name: Hill Sprint
original.risk: All-out incline sprinting drives a maximal calf/Achilles and hamstring push that can shock cranky 40+ lower-leg tissue.
original.regression: Use Hill Stride (submaximal, clean mechanics) on a moderate grade instead of the all-out effort until well-conditioned.

verdict.name: Hill Sprint
verdict.rationale: The "how" is explicitly maximal/all-out, loading cranky 40+ calf/Achilles/hamstring tissue hard; AMBER+regress keeps a valuable movement while capping intensity.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Use Hill Stride (submaximal, clean mechanics) on a moderate grade instead of the all-out effort until well-conditioned.

original.name: 30-Sec Shuttle
original.risk: Repeated max sprint-decelerate-turn cycles for 30s hammer the knees and groin and cook hamstrings as fatigue sets in.
original.regression: Lengthen the shuttle distance to soften the turns, shorten the bout, and run at ~80% so decelerations stay controlled.

verdict.name: 30-Sec Shuttle
verdict.rationale: Real risk: 30s of continuous max sprint-plant-turn cycles loads knees/groin and degrades hamstring mechanics under accumulating fatigue — exactly when masters soft-tissue strains happen; regress (not exclude) keeps a valuable CoD skill in scaled form.
verdict.suggestedRegression: Lengthen the shuttle distance so the turns are gentler and there are fewer of them, cut the bout to ~15-20s, and run at ~80% so you can brake under control — if a turn pinches a knee or groin, round it off into a wide arc instead of a hard plant, or swap to a straight tempo run/15s sprint interval with no cut.

items.name: Suitcase Get-Up
items.risk: The loaded floor-to-stand transition through a deep kneel and lunge under an offset load adds knee, low-back, and balance demand that can aggravate cranky joints.
items.regression: If it bothers your knee or back, do the DB Suitcase Carry instead (same anti-tip stimulus, no floor transition); or lighten the load and limit the range to half-kneeling-to-stand.

items.name: Carry Complex (Farmer + Suitcase + Overhead)
items.risk: The sustained loaded overhead-carry segment stresses cranky shoulders and can drive low-back arching if shoulder/t-spine mobility won't allow a clean overhead position.
items.regression: If overhead bothers your shoulder or back, drop that segment and do the Farmer + Suitcase Complex instead; or keep the load light and short and hold the weight in a front-rack carry rather than overhead.

original.name: Suitcase Get-Up
original.risk: The loaded floor-to-stand transition through a deep kneel and lunge under an offset load adds knee, low-back, and balance demand that can aggravate cranky joints.
original.regression: If it bothers your knee or back, do the DB Suitcase Carry instead (same anti-tip stimulus, no floor transition); or lighten the load and limit the range to half-kneeling-to-stand.

verdict.name: Suitcase Get-Up
verdict.rationale: Loaded deep kneel/lunge, eccentric floor descent, and offset spinal load genuinely stack knee/back/balance demand for worn 40+ joints; AMBER+regress (not RED) is correct since the anti-tip value is retained via a scaled carry.
verdict.suggestedRegression: If it bothers your knee or back, do the DB Suitcase Carry instead (same anti-tip/anti-lean stimulus, no floor transition); or lighten the load and limit the range to half-kneeling-to-stand.

original.name: Carry Complex (Farmer + Suitcase + Overhead)
original.risk: The sustained loaded overhead-carry segment stresses cranky shoulders and can drive low-back arching if shoulder/t-spine mobility won't allow a clean overhead position.
original.regression: If overhead bothers your shoulder or back, drop that segment and do the Farmer + Suitcase Complex instead; or keep the load light and short and hold the weight in a front-rack carry rather than overhead.

verdict.name: Carry Complex (Farmer + Suitcase + Overhead)
verdict.rationale: Real risk is the loaded overhead-hold segment (end-range shoulder + lumbar arching for cranky-shouldered masters); farmer/suitcase segments are great, so regress (not exclude) is correct.
verdict.suggestedRegression: If overhead bothers your shoulder or back, drop that segment and do the Farmer + Suitcase Complex instead (it exists in the pool — same carry minus the overhead); or keep the load light and short and hold the weight in a front-rack carry rather than overhead.

items.name: Rotational Med Ball Toss
items.risk: Ballistic free-release hip-and-trunk whip loads the lumbar discs and obliques at speed, which can tweak a cranky 40+ back if rushed.
items.regression: Swap to Banded Rotational Press for an eccentrically-controlled return, or per the 'how' text reduce wind-up depth and rotate sub-maximally, building speed only once the back stays quiet.

items.name: Rotational Med Ball Side Toss
items.risk: The low, scooping ballistic side release adds a side-bend/rotation combination that is a common oblique and low-back strain pattern for older trunks.

items.name: Banded Rotational Throw (Wall)
items.risk: The 'explosively rotate and release' / ballistic follow-through cue invites a max-speed trunk snap that can aggravate the lower back at full intent.
items.regression: Default to the controlled Banded Rotational Press (drive the rotation but keep tension and control the return) rather than a ballistic release; if it bothers the back, slow the tempo and reduce rotational range per the 'how'.

original.name: Rotational Med Ball Toss

verdict.name: Rotational Med Ball Toss

If it still bothers your back, swap to the pool's Banded Rotational Press — same hip-driven transverse pattern but no free-release and an explicitly controlled eccentric return, removing the ballistic spike and the catch/deceleration shock.

items.name: DB Thruster

items.name: DB Complex
