designs.philosophy: Linear durability-first. A classic linear wave built around one rule for the 40+ athlete: tissue tolerance and recovery are the limiter, not work capacity. Block 1 (weeks 1-4) rebuilds the chassis - mobility, joint prep, movement quality, and tendon-prep strength through full but controlled ranges - so nothing later is loaded on top of a stiff or under-prepared joint. Block 2 (weeks 5-8) layers moderate-amplitude power and joint-friendly hockey conditioning onto that rebuilt base. Volume waves up to a single peak, then unloads twice on purpose: a genuine deload at Week 4 to bank the first block's gains, and a true recovery-emphasis taper at Week 8 that leaves the player fresher and more durable than when they started. Three sessions a week, 45-60 minutes, every single one bookended by mobility and recovery as headline work - never as warm-up filler. The whole model is self-coachable by an adult with no S&C background: quality and consistency beat load and volume at every decision point, and every hard category is dialed back before it can pick a fight with a cranky knee, back, shoulder, or Achilles.

blocks.name: Foundation & Tissue Prep
blocks.theme: Rebuild movement quality, mobility, and joint/tendon readiness. Re-groove hinge, squat, lunge and carry patterns through controlled full ranges; build tendon-prep strength with bodyweight, bands, and light dumbbells. No power, no impact - earn the right to load later. Volume ramps gently to a Week 3 high, then a genuine Week 4 deload locks in the gains.

blocks.name: Moderate Power & Hockey Engine
blocks.theme: Layer moderate-amplitude power (low-box step-downs, soft-landing hops, low-volume jumps) and joint-friendly hockey conditioning onto the rebuilt base. Strength holds steady while expressing it faster and adding intervals/carries that mimic shift demands. Peak at Week 7, then a true Week 8 taper that emphasizes recovery and leaves the player fresher than at the start.

weeks.label: PREP
weeks.emphasis: Mobility & movement quality - re-learn the patterns
weeks.notes: Ease in. Own bodyweight hinge, squat, lunge, and a daily mobility flow before any load - if a range pinches, shorten it, never force it.

weeks.label: BUILD
weeks.emphasis: Tendon-prep strength - slow tempo, full control
weeks.notes: Add light dumbbells/bands with slow lowering tempos to load tendons gently. Keep 2-3 reps in the tank; carries and anti-rotation core lead. Regress range if a joint complains.

weeks.label: BUILD (peak of Block 1)
weeks.emphasis: Strength quality at the block's highest volume
weeks.notes: Highest workload of Block 1 - one more set or a touch more weight, never to failure. Still no impact or power. This is as hard as Block 1 gets.

weeks.label: DELOAD
weeks.emphasis: Genuine deload - absorb and recover
weeks.notes: Real deload, not a token one: fewer sets, lighter loads, extra mobility and easy movement. You should finish each session feeling you could do more - that is the point.

weeks.label: BUILD / INTRODUCE
weeks.emphasis: Introduce moderate-amplitude power & low-impact speed
weeks.notes: First power work: low-box step-downs, soft pogo hops, low-volume jumps with quiet landings. Quality of landing over height. Strength holds; mobility still leads every session.

weeks.label: BUILD
weeks.emphasis: Power + hockey conditioning come together
weeks.notes: Add joint-friendly intervals and change-of-direction at sub-max speed to build the shift engine. Keep jump volume low and amplitude moderate - protect the Achilles and knees.

weeks.label: PEAK
weeks.emphasis: Peak expression - moderate power at sharpest quality
weeks.notes: The program's high point: most complete sessions, power expressed crisply, conditioning closest to game demands. High quality, NOT high impact - no grinding, no max plyo, soft landings only.

weeks.label: TAPER / RECOVER
weeks.emphasis: True taper - shed fatigue, stay fresh & durable
weeks.notes: Extended recovery taper for the 40+ body: short, sharp, low-volume sessions keep the nervous system primed while fatigue clears. Headline the mobility and recovery work - finish the block fresher than you started.

designs.rationale: This model is engineered for an athlete whose ceiling is set by tissue tolerance and recovery, not by how much work they can grind through - so it spends its first four weeks earning the right to do anything explosive. Block 1 treats cranky knees, backs, shoulders, and Achilles as the normal starting condition and rebuilds the patterns and tendon readiness around them before a single jump or sprint appears. By the time moderate-amplitude power and hockey conditioning arrive in Block 2, they land on a chassis that has been re-prepped through full controlled ranges, which is exactly how you keep an older athlete on the ice instead of in rehab. The linear wave is deliberately conservative: volume rises to a single modest peak (Week 7), intensity never reaches maximal, power is capped at moderate amplitude with soft landings, and nothing is taken to failure - quality and consistency are the progression, not load. Two real off-ramps protect the gains and the joints: a genuine Week 4 deload that banks Block 1's adaptations before stacking power on top, and a true Week 8 recovery taper - deliberately more recovery than a younger program would get - that clears accumulated fatigue so the player ends the eight weeks fresher, more mobile, and more durable than they began. Mobility, joint prep, and recovery headline every week rather than hiding in the warm-up, which is the single biggest lever for on-ice longevity in the 40+ player and the reason this plan is safe to self-coach and easy to repeat as a sustainable training cycle.

designs.philosophy: Daily-undulating for the 40+ body. Each week holds three fixed-flavour sessions that recur all eight weeks: Day A = Strength & Quality (technique-led hinge/squat/single-leg, carries, anti-rotation core), Day B = Speed & Reactive (low-impact linear/lateral mechanics, agility, soft-landing footwork), Day C = Power & Compete (moderate-amplitude jumps, light ballistic intent, joint-friendly conditioning, small-sided compete finish). Because the hard, the fast, and the springy never land on back-to-back days, no single tissue — cranky knee, low back, shoulder, Achilles — is hit two days running. We undulate intensity WITHIN the week and progress conservatively ACROSS the weeks, biasing quality and consistency over load or volume. Every session opens with mobility and joint prep as the headline, not the warm-up afterthought, and closes with deliberate recovery. The limiter is tissue tolerance and recovery, not work capacity, so the whole model is tuned for staying on the ice for years, not peaking once.

blocks.name: GROOVE & PREP
blocks.theme: Re-establish joint prep, movement quality, and the three day-flavours at low amplitude. Build mobility, single-leg control, controlled speed mechanics, and bodyweight/band strength on durable tissue. Deload at Week 4.

blocks.name: EXPRESS & COMPETE
blocks.theme: Same three day-flavours, slightly sharper. Add moderate-amplitude power, crisper reactive work, and small-sided compete — always with soft landings and capped volume. Taper hard at Week 8 with recovery emphasis.

weeks.label: PREP
weeks.emphasis: Mobility & joint prep first; learn the three day-flavours at easy amplitude
weeks.notes: On-ramp week. Groove the patterns, own every landing soft, leave reps in the tank everywhere.

weeks.label: BUILD
weeks.emphasis: Movement quality & single-leg control; nudge strength-day load
weeks.notes: Add a touch of range and a rep or two on Strength day; speed and power stay submaximal and clean.

weeks.label: BUILD
weeks.emphasis: Block-1 working peak — best mechanics on all three days
weeks.notes: Busiest week of Block 1, still conservative. Sharpen speed mechanics; power amplitude stays modest.

weeks.label: DELOAD
weeks.emphasis: Recovery & mobility headline; re-check the movement screen
weeks.notes: Genuine deload — cut sets ~40-50%, drop impact, keep it crisp. Let the work catch up and re-run your checkpoints.

weeks.label: BUILD
weeks.emphasis: Re-enter sharp; introduce moderate-amplitude power with soft landings
weeks.notes: Slightly higher than W2. Power day gains a low-box/short-amplitude jump; carries and core stay strong staples.

weeks.label: BUILD
weeks.emphasis: Reactive speed & change-of-direction quality; light ballistic intent
weeks.notes: Crisper cuts and footwork on Speed day. No max-impact plyo, no grinding — moderate amplitude only.

weeks.label: PEAK
weeks.emphasis: Program peak — express power and compete with the best quality
weeks.notes: Highest of the program but still capped: top (moderate) amplitude, small-sided compete finish, perfect landings. This is as hard as it gets.

weeks.label: TAPER
weeks.emphasis: Recovery emphasis & mobility; volume drops, quality stays; final checkpoint
weeks.notes: Real taper — more recovery than a younger program. Halve volume, keep movements sharp and brief, re-run checkpoints, arrive fresh.

designs.rationale: For a competitive 40+ rec player, the constraint that drives every decision is not how much work you can do but how fast tissue recovers and how long it stays healthy — so the model is built around durability and on-ice longevity, not a one-time peak. Three things make it 40+-appropriate. (1) Daily undulation is the safety mechanism: by giving each weekly session a fixed flavour (Strength, Speed, Power) that recurs all eight weeks, a cranky knee, low back, shoulder, or Achilles never gets hammered on consecutive days — there are always 48+ hours and a different stress before that tissue is loaded hard again, which is exactly what aging connective tissue needs to adapt without flaring. (2) Conservative, quality-first progression: volume and intensity climb only gently and stay capped (Block-1 peaks at "moderate," the whole program peaks at "moderate-to-high" in Week 7), power is expressed at moderate amplitude with soft landings and low volume throughout — no max-impact plyo, no grinding to failure, no deep loaded ranges — so we accumulate movement skill and connective-tissue resilience rather than chasing numbers the body can't safely absorb. (3) Recovery is structural, not optional: mobility and joint prep headline every single week (not just warm-ups), and there are TWO full unloading weeks — a genuine deload at Week 4 and a deeper taper with recovery emphasis at Week 8 — giving the program a lower overall stress-to-recovery ratio than a younger band would need. Two clean four-week blocks let the player re-check their movement at W4 and W8, reinforcing the real goal: keep moving well, keep playing, and stay on the ice for many more seasons.

designs.philosophy: Mobility-forward minimalist for the 40+ competitive rec player. The headline every single week is joint prep, tissue tolerance, and movement quality — not warm-up filler, the main event. Strength and power are kept deliberately low-volume and high-quality: bodyweight, bands, and light dumbbells only, power expressed at MODERATE amplitude with soft landings and low rep counts to protect tendons, knees, backs, shoulders, and Achilles. The governing limiter is assumed to be tissue tolerance and recovery, not work capacity, so weekly volume stays below a younger band's and quality always outranks load. Progression is conservative and earns its way up: we add a little crispness and range before we ever add load or volume. Two four-week blocks, with a genuine deload at Week 4 and a real recovery-emphasis taper at Week 8 that gives back more than a younger program would — built so the player stays on the ice for years, not so he peaks once.

blocks.name: PREP & CAPACITY
blocks.theme: Restore range and load tolerance first. Re-pattern hinge, single-leg, and core control; teach controlled deceleration and low-amplitude footwork; build a gentle aerobic and carry base. Volume rises modestly W1 to W3 (a low peak), then W4 deloads and you re-check your movement. Mobility and joint prep lead every session.

blocks.name: EXPRESS & COMPETE
blocks.theme: Turn that durable base into usable, joint-friendly power and reactive sharpness — moderate-amplitude jumps with soft landings, short capped sprints, change-of-direction, and shift-like intervals. Volume rises modestly W5 to W7 (a low peak), then W8 tapers hard into a recovery-emphasis week and a final movement re-check. Power is always capped; nothing grinds to failure.

weeks.label: PREP
weeks.emphasis: Joint prep & movement re-patterning — re-learn the hinge, single-leg stance, and braced core
weeks.notes: Easiest week on purpose: groove clean technique and full pain-free range before adding anything. If a joint barks, shorten the range — don't push through.

weeks.label: BUILD
weeks.emphasis: Mobility-led base build — add light loaded carries, tempo bodyweight strength, easy aerobic work
weeks.notes: Same movements, a touch more work. Carries and bands do the heavy lifting; keep every rep smooth and a couple shy of failure.

weeks.label: PEAK
weeks.emphasis: Block-1 peak — most volume of the block, controlled deceleration and low-amplitude footwork added
weeks.notes: Busiest week of Block 1, but still moderate — this is a masters peak, not a young athlete's. Footwork and decel stay low and crisp, not max-effort.

weeks.label: DELOAD
weeks.emphasis: Genuine deload — pull volume back, move clean, re-run your movement checkpoints
weeks.notes: Real unload: roughly half the work, easy quality only. This is recovery, not a grind — let the first block catch up and re-check how you move.

weeks.label: BUILD
weeks.emphasis: Mobility maintained, introduce moderate-amplitude power — low pogos and skater bounds with soft 'stick' landings
weeks.notes: First real power, capped and soft-landed. Low jump volume, long rests — quality over quantity. Knees/Achilles cranky? Sub low-box step-downs for the jumps.

weeks.label: BUILD
weeks.emphasis: Reactive sharpness — short capped sprints, change-of-direction, anti-rotation core under speed
weeks.notes: Add short accelerations and cuts, all sub-maximal and well-rested. Run on grass/turf to spare joints. No deep loaded ranges, no ballistic shock.

weeks.label: PEAK
weeks.emphasis: Block-2 peak — integrate power, speed, and shift-like joint-friendly intervals (the busiest week)
weeks.notes: Highest output of the program, still capped: moderate amplitude, soft landings, intervals that mimic shifts without pounding tissue. Stop a rep before form slips.

weeks.label: TAPER / RECOVER
weeks.emphasis: Recovery-emphasis taper — volume drops sharply, mobility and easy quality lead, final movement re-check
weeks.notes: Deeper unload than a younger program: keep a little snap so you stay sharp, but the week is mostly mobility, carries, and recovery. Re-test against Week 0 and feel fresh.

designs.rationale: This model is built around the one assumption that defines a 40+ competitive rec player: the limiter is tissue tolerance and recovery, not work capacity. So mobility and joint prep are the headline of every week rather than a warm-up afterthought, and the whole macro is front-loaded toward restoring and protecting range before any meaningful load or impact appears. Block 1 (PREP & CAPACITY) spends four full weeks earning tissue tolerance — re-patterning the hinge, single-leg control, and braced core, layering in loaded carries (ideal for this athlete: high training value, near-zero joint shock) and a gentle aerobic base — so that when power finally arrives in Block 2 it lands on prepared, durable tissue instead of a cranky joint. Power is intentionally withheld until Week 5 and never exceeds moderate amplitude with soft 'stick' landings and low rep counts, which is the single biggest lever for protecting aging knees, Achilles, and backs from the ballistic shock that injures masters athletes. The two peaks (W3 and W7) are deliberately low relative to a younger band — a masters peak, not a teenager's — because chasing output is exactly how accumulated wear flares. The genuine W4 deload (roughly half volume, easy quality) lets the first block consolidate, and the W8 taper gives back more recovery than a younger program would: it is a recovery-emphasis week that retains just enough snap to stay sharp while the body fully absorbs the work. Conservative, quality-over-load progression (a little more crispness and range before any added weight or volume), a plain-language regression on every hard element, and built-in unloads twice in eight weeks all serve the real goal for this athlete — not a one-time peak, but staying healthy and on the ice season after season.

designs.philosophy: Concurrent hockey-transfer for the 40-plus masters athlete. Every single week trains a little of EVERYTHING skating needs: mobility and joint prep, single-leg strength, hinge, lateral push-off, anti-rotation, groin care, loaded carries, and moderate-amplitude power, biased toward direct on-ice transfer and staying game-ready year-round. Nothing is saved for a later block; there is no specialization phase. What changes across the 8 weeks is the dial, not the menu. Weekly volume stays deliberately low-to-moderate and roughly flat, while INTENSITY (crispness, expression, reactive demand, compete sharpness, and amplitude within a moderate joint-friendly cap) is the variable that ramps to a peak. For a body with accumulated wear, the limiter is tissue tolerance and recovery, not work capacity, so we cycle intensity up while keeping volume conservative, and we headline recovery, joint prep, and mobility every week as the main event, never a warm-up afterthought. Block 1 builds the tissue and movement foundation that lets us safely turn the intensity dial; Block 2 expresses moderate power and competes, then unloads into a recovery-weighted taper. Power is always moderate amplitude with soft landings and low volume: no max-impact plyo, no grinding to failure, no deep loaded ranges.

blocks.name: FOUNDATION AND TISSUE PREP
blocks.theme: Earn the right to go fast. Re-pattern landing, deceleration, single-leg balance, the hinge, groin, and anti-rotation, and prepare tendons, ankles, hips, and back to tolerate intensity. The full concurrent menu is present from day one but kept at moderate, technique-led intensity while tissue tolerance is built. Intensity creeps up from week 1 to week 3, then week 4 is a genuine deload and joint-prep reset.

blocks.name: EXPRESS AND COMPETE
blocks.theme: Turn the dial up, safely. Same concurrent menu, now expressed with more crispness, more reactive change-of-direction, and moderate-amplitude power, finished with competitive play that mimics shifts. Intensity, never volume, climbs to a true peak at weeks 6 and 7, then week 8 is a recovery-weighted taper: short, sharp, and game-ready, with more rest than a younger program would take.

weeks.label: PREP
weeks.emphasis: Joint prep and re-pattern: mobility, single-leg balance, clean landings, groin and anti-rotation
weeks.notes: Ease in. The whole concurrent menu is present but light. Own the positions and grease every pattern before any dial moves. Regress anything that bites a knee, back, or shoulder by shrinking the range or using the easier option.

weeks.label: BUILD
weeks.emphasis: Movement quality: hinge technique, single-leg strength, lateral push-off, daily mobility
weeks.notes: Same menu, a touch sharper. Add controlled tempo to the hinge and single-leg work, and introduce light carries. Quality and pain-free range are the only progressions. Never add load if the reps are not clean today.

weeks.label: BUILD-PLUS
weeks.emphasis: Controlled speed and moderate power introduced: crisper push-off, soft-landing low jumps
weeks.volume: moderate, the block 1 high-water mark but still conservative
weeks.notes: Busiest week of Block 1, but volume stays capped. The increase is intensity and crispness, not piling on sets. First moderate-amplitude jumps with soft, stuck landings and low contact counts. Cranky joints today means keep the strength and mobility and skip the jumps.

weeks.label: DELOAD
weeks.emphasis: Genuine unload and joint-prep reset: mobility, breathing, balance, light movement
weeks.volume: low, volume cut about 40 to 50 percent
weeks.notes: A real deload, not a token light day. Pull sets and impact back hard, keep the full mobility, groin, and anti-rotation menu, and let accumulated load settle. Re-run your movement checkpoints. This is where the foundation consolidates.

weeks.label: EXPRESS
weeks.emphasis: Express the foundation: moderate power, reactive single-leg, lateral bounds with soft landings
weeks.notes: Dial up expression, not volume. Same concurrent menu, now with a bit more pop and reactive demand. Carries and groin work stay headline. Caps on jump amplitude and contacts hold to protect the tendons and Achilles.

weeks.label: PEAK
weeks.emphasis: Peak intensity: crispest power, sharpest change-of-direction, shift-style compete
weeks.volume: moderate, matched to week 3 and held there
weeks.intensity: high, the top of the program, moderate amplitude only
weeks.notes: The intensity peak. Power is at its most expressive but still moderate amplitude with soft landings and full rest, never max-impact and never to failure. Volume is held at the week 3 ceiling; only the dial is up. A cranky day means drop a set and keep the quality.

weeks.label: INTEGRATE
weeks.emphasis: Integrate: speed, moderate power, single-leg strength, and compete in one sharp session
weeks.volume: low-to-moderate, trimming as the taper begins
weeks.intensity: high, held then easing late in the week
weeks.notes: Hold the sharpness, start trimming volume. Put the qualities together and compete with intent. This is what staying game-ready looks like. Begin backing volume down into the taper while keeping every rep crisp.

weeks.label: TAPER / RECOVER
weeks.emphasis: Recovery-weighted taper: stay sharp, restore tissue, mobility and easy compete
weeks.volume: very low, volume cut about 50 to 60 percent
weeks.intensity: low-to-moderate, a few crisp efforts then recover
weeks.notes: More recovery than a younger program would take. A handful of sharp, short efforts to stay primed, then heavy emphasis on mobility, breathing, sleep, and tissue care. Re-test your checkpoints, see the gains, and walk in game-ready, not buried.

designs.rationale: This model is built around the one fact that governs the 40-plus masters athlete: the limiter is tissue tolerance and recovery, not work capacity, and that fact drives every choice. First, it inverts the usual youth periodization. Instead of climbing volume to a peak, which is how older joints, tendons, and the lower back get overcooked, it keeps weekly volume deliberately low-to-moderate and roughly flat and ramps intensity and expression to the peak instead. Crispness and moderate-amplitude power tax the nervous system but spare the tissues, exactly the trade a worn body should make. Second, the concurrent structure keeps the athlete game-ready the entire 8 weeks. Because mobility, single-leg, hinge, lateral push-off, anti-rotation, groin care, carries, and moderate power all appear every week, no skating quality ever goes untrained and detrains, which is critical for a rec player who may be playing through the program and cannot afford a four-week off-ice-only power block that leaves them stiff on the ice. Third, two genuine unloads protect longevity. Week 4 is a real deload with volume cut 40 to 50 percent, impact pulled, and mobility kept, which lets the first three weeks settle and consolidate, and Week 8 is a recovery-weighted taper with more rest than a younger program takes, so the athlete arrives sharp and fresh rather than fatigued. Fourth, recovery, joint prep, and mobility are written as the weekly headline emphasis, not the warm-up, because for this population that work is the main driver of staying on the ice. Fifth, power is capped at moderate amplitude with soft landings, low contact counts, and full rest throughout, with no max-impact plyo, no grinding, and no deep loaded ranges, and every prescription is paired with a plain-language regression so a cranky knee, back, shoulder, or Achilles is designed around, not pushed through. The net effect is measurable on-ice sharpness and durability without the February tendon flare or pulled hamstring that ends a masters player's season: training built for season three, five, and ten, not just this one.

scores.lens: Tissue tolerance, recovery margin & injury risk for a 40+ athlete with accumulated wear
ranking: Mobility-forward minimalist for the 40+ competitive rec player.
Concurrent hockey-transfer for the 40-plus masters athlete.

scores.comment: Best fit for the lens. Its core safety mechanism IS tissue management: fixed day-flavours (Strength/Speed/Power) recurring all 8 weeks guarantee a cranky knee/back/shoulder/Achilles is never hammered on consecutive days, giving 48+ hrs and a different stress before any tissue is reloaded hard. This is the single most important variable for aging connective tissue and no other model engineers it as explicitly. Genuine W4 deload (cut sets ~40-50%, drop impact) and a real W8 taper (halve volume, lowest of program, recovery emphasis) are both present and honest. Progression is capped (Block 1 peaks 'moderate', program peaks 'moderate-to-high' at W7), power is moderate-amplitude/soft-landing/low-volume throughout, mobility headlines every week, and W4/W8 movement re-checks add a self-coachable feedback loop. Minor knock: 'Power & Compete' Day C stacks moderate jumps + ballistic intent + small-sided compete in one session, which is the densest single-session tissue load in the program and could spike on a peak week; an explicit 'cranky day = drop the compete finish' regression would tighten it. Not a 10 only because that one day is the program's least-protected moment.

scores.philosophy: Mobility-forward minimalist for the 40+ competitive rec player.
scores.comment: Most conservative on raw tissue dosing and arguably the safest in absolute load terms. Power is deliberately withheld until Week 5 and never exceeds moderate amplitude with soft 'stick' landings and low reps; loaded carries (near-zero joint shock, high value) are used generously; both peaks (W3, W7) are explicitly 'a masters peak, not a teenager's'. Genuine W4 deload (~half volume) and a deeper-than-younger W8 taper are both real. Every hard element carries a plain-language regression ('cranky knees/Achilles? sub low-box step-downs'), which is exactly the lens requirement. Slightly behind the DUP model on ONE axis only: it does not structurally separate the hard/fast/springy stresses within the week, so within Block 2 a single week can load impact-adjacent work on closer-spaced days than DUP guarantees. The minimalist low total volume is protective but the model leans on the athlete to self-space sessions rather than building the spacing into the template. Strong, durable, and very self-coachable.

Genuinely durability-led and sound: four full weeks of chassis/tendon prep before ANY power or impact appears (no jumps/sprints until W5) is the most tissue-protective on-ramp of the four, and it earns the right to load. Honest W4 deload ('finish feeling you could do more') and a true extended W8 recovery taper. Caps on amplitude, no failure, mobility as headline all present. Two structural concerns under this lens. (1) The single linear peak at W7 concentrates the program's highest combined volume+intensity into one window late in the cycle; a linear wave inherently rides higher accumulated fatigue into that peak than an undulating or flat-volume model, which is the classic over-cook risk for worn tissue. (2) Block 1 front-loads almost all prep and Block 2 front-loads almost all impact, so once power arrives it recurs across W5-W7 without the within-week tissue rotation the DUP model provides. Excellent first block; the back half carries marginally more linear-fatigue risk than the top two. Solidly safe, just less elegantly de-risked.

scores.philosophy: Concurrent hockey-transfer for the 40-plus masters athlete.
scores.comment: Smart core idea for the lens (hold volume flat, ramp INTENSITY not volume, sparing tissue while taxing the nervous system) and it has both real unloads: W4 deload (cut 40-50%, impact pulled) and a recovery-weighted W8 taper (cut 50-60%). Power is capped, regressions are written in, mobility headlines every week. But it scores lowest because it carries the most tissue risk of the four under THIS specific lens. (1) It runs a TWO-week high-intensity plateau (W6 PEAK + W7 INTEGRATE both 'high'), and back-to-back high-intensity weeks with no intervening unload is the riskiest accumulation pattern for a 40+ body even at capped amplitude. (2) The concurrent menu trains 'a little of everything every week,' which by design means impact-adjacent and ballistic-intent work appears every week with the LEAST inter-stress spacing of any model, the opposite of the DUP separation principle. (3) W7 explicitly puts speed + power + single-leg + compete 'in one sharp session' at high intensity, the single highest-collision session in any of the four candidates. The flat-volume/rising-intensity thesis is genuinely clever and game-readiness is a real benefit, but two consecutive high weeks plus minimal intra-week tissue rotation under-protects recovery margin relative to the field.

scores.bestDesign: Daily-undulating for the 40+ body. It is the only model that builds tissue protection directly into the weekly architecture rather than relying on overall conservatism: fixed Strength/Speed/Power day-flavours that recur all eight weeks mathematically prevent any single vulnerable tissue (knee, back, shoulder, Achilles) from being loaded hard on consecutive days, always leaving 48+ hours and an intervening different stress before that tissue is re-challenged. For a population whose limiter is recovery rate and connective-tissue tolerance, that inter-stress spacing is the highest-leverage injury-risk reducer available, and it pairs that with everything the lens demands: a genuine ~40-50% W4 deload, a real volume-halving recovery-emphasis W8 taper (the lowest of the program), capped moderate-amplitude/soft-landing power throughout, mobility headlining every week, and W4/W8 movement re-checks for self-coaching. It achieves maximum recovery margin without forcing the player off the ice, which the minimalist model approaches in safety but does not structurally guarantee.
scores.bestIdeasFromOthers: 1) From the Mobility-forward minimalist: withhold power entirely until Week 5 and lean harder on loaded carries (near-zero joint shock, high training value) as the primary 'strength' driver — grafting this onto the DUP winner would make its early Power-day work even gentler. Also adopt its explicit per-element regression on EVERY hard movement (e.g., 'cranky knees/Achilles? sub low-box step-downs for the jumps'), which is more thoroughly written-in than the DUP model's. 2) From the Linear durability-first model: its four-week dedicated tendon-prep on-ramp with slow eccentric tempos before any impact — the DUP winner introduces all three flavours from Week 1, so borrowing a stricter 'no real load until tendons are prepped' gate for the first two weeks would lower early-cycle risk. 3) From the Concurrent model: the flat-volume / ramp-intensity-only thesis (rising crispness and expression rather than rising sets) is an excellent way to peak a worn body without accumulating volume fatigue — worth importing as the progression rule INSIDE each DUP day, while keeping DUP's superior inter-day tissue separation and avoiding the Concurrent model's two consecutive high-intensity weeks. 4) Universally adopt the explicit W4/W8 movement re-check / re-test-against-Week-0 checkpoints (present in the undulating, minimalist, and concurrent models) as a self-coachable durability feedback loop.

scores.lens: On-ice hockey transfer & staying game-ready through a rec season
ranking: Concurrent hockey-transfer for the 40-plus masters athlete
Mobility-forward minimalist for the 40+ competitive rec player

scores.philosophy: Concurrent hockey-transfer for the 40-plus masters athlete
scores.comment: Best fit for THIS lens by a clear margin. The whole menu (single-leg, lateral push-off, groin, anti-rotation, carries, moderate power, compete) is trained EVERY week from day one, so no skating quality detrains while the player is in-season and playing games. That is the single most important property for staying game-ready through a rec season: a guy playing Tuesday-night hockey can't afford a 4-week block where power or reactive work goes dark. Holding volume flat and ramping intensity/expression is also exactly right for in-season transfer (sharpens the nervous system, spares tissue, keeps the player crisp on the ice rather than buried under accumulated volume). Lateral push-off, groin care and reactive single-leg are explicitly named and recur weekly — these are the highest-transfer skating qualities and this is the only design that protects all of them continuously. Genuine W4 deload (40-50% cut, impact pulled) and a recovery-weighted W8 taper (50-60% cut) are both present and real. Compete/shift-style work appears by W6-7 to bridge to the ice. Loses a point because intensity sits at 'high' across W6-W7 with compete on top — for a true in-season player ALSO playing games, that's the one window where total CNS/joint load could collide with game night; a 'cranky-day' regression is noted but in-season game scheduling isn't explicitly coordinated.

Strong for this lens and the best of the block-periodized field. The three fixed flavours (Strength / Speed & Reactive / Power & Compete) recur all eight weeks, so like the concurrent model it keeps every quality alive in-season rather than parking one for a block — good for game-readiness and transfer. Speed/reactive mechanics and a small-sided compete finish are baked in weekly, which is high on-ice carryover. The DUP spacing (hard/fast/springy never back-to-back) is genuinely valuable for a 40+ body that's also absorbing game collisions and shifts mid-week. Real W4 deload and a real W8 taper. Slightly behind concurrent only because it's organized around three discrete day-types rather than touching every skating quality every session; if a busy rec player misses a day, an entire quality (e.g., the only reactive/speed day) is missed that week — concurrent is more robust to a missed session, which matters for real-world in-season adherence.

scores.philosophy: Mobility-forward minimalist for the 40+ competitive rec player
scores.comment: Safe and durable, but weaker specifically on transfer/game-readiness because it is block-sequenced: power and reactive sharpness are deliberately WITHHELD until Week 5. For an in-season rec player that means the first four weeks do little to maintain the explosive/reactive qualities they need on the ice right now — those qualities detrain or stay dormant through the entire first block. That's the classic mismatch for a player who is playing games throughout. The minimalist menu (bodyweight/bands/light DBs, carries) is appropriate and self-coachable, and the deload/taper are genuine, but withholding speed and power for half the program costs real on-ice transfer in-season. Footwork/decel are also kept 'low and crisp' deep into Block 1, so reactive carryover ramps late. Good longevity model, middling game-ready model.

Lowest on THIS lens despite being a perfectly sound durability program. The fatal flaw for hockey transfer in-season: NO power and NO impact/speed at all for the first four full weeks ('No power, no impact - earn the right to load later'). A 40+ rec player who is playing games the whole time will detrain reactive and explosive skating qualities for half the program, and arrives at the ice in the first block having trained none of the qualities a shift actually demands (acceleration, change of direction, lateral push-off, reactive landings). It's the most rehab-flavoured of the four. Conditioning that mimics shift demands doesn't appear until W6, and CoD/lateral work is sparse throughout. Deload (W4) and taper (W8) are genuine and well-described, and tissue prep is excellent — but as an in-season, stay-game-ready, on-ice-transfer model it's the weakest because it sequences away the very qualities the ice needs and leaves them untrained for the first 4 weeks.

scores.bestDesign: Concurrent hockey-transfer for the 40-plus masters athlete
scores.bestIdeasFromOthers: From Daily-undulating: graft its hard/fast/springy-never-on-consecutive-days SPACING rule onto the concurrent model — this is the cleanest way to dilute the W6-W7 'high intensity + compete' collision that is the concurrent model's one weakness for a player also playing games mid-week; spreading the springy and reactive stress across non-adjacent days protects a 40+ body better than cramming qualities into one concurrent session. Also graft DUP's explicit small-sided COMPETE FINISH as a recurring weekly element (not just W6-7) — competitive play is the most direct on-ice transfer tool and keeping a low-key compete finish every week maximizes game-readiness. From Mobility-forward minimalist: graft its explicit, per-element plain-language REGRESSIONS tied to specific joints ('knees/Achilles cranky? sub low-box step-downs for the jumps'; 'run on grass/turf to spare joints') and its W0-vs-W8 MOVEMENT RE-CHECK/CHECKPOINT loop — concrete self-coachable off-ramps and a re-test bookend make the program safer and more repeatable season to season. From Linear durability-first: graft its discipline of headlining mobility/recovery as the actual main event bookending every session, and its framing of a 'genuine deload you finish feeling you could do more' — a crisp, unambiguous deload instruction that an adult with no S&C background can self-administer correctly. Crucially, do NOT graft Linear's or Minimalist's withhold-power-for-Block-1 sequencing — that is exactly the property that hurts in-season on-ice transfer and game-readiness."

scores.lens: Adherence to 40+ programming rules: explicit 3x/wk structure, a REAL Week-4 deload and Week-8 taper (not token), recovery/mobility/joint-prep as the weekly headline, conservative quality-over-load progression, and strict impact control (moderate-amplitude power only, soft landings, no max plyo, no grinding to failure). Designs were penalized for under-dosed recovery, token unloads, early/aggressive power or impact, and back-to-back high-intensity weeks; rewarded for withholding power until tissue is prepped, deep well-specified unloads, day-to-day tissue sequencing, and built-in regressions.
ranking: Mobility-forward minimalist for the 40+ competitive rec player
Concurrent hockey-transfer for the 40-plus masters athlete

scores.comment: Strongest fit for this lens. Explicit 3x/wk via fixed Day A/B/C flavours that recur all 8 weeks, and the fixed-flavour structure is the single best TISSUE-PROTECTION mechanism of the four: a cranky knee/back/shoulder/Achilles is never loaded hard on consecutive days (48+ hrs between same-tissue stress), which directly executes the 'design around cranky joints' rule rather than just asserting it. Genuine W4 deload (cut sets ~40-50%, drop impact) and a real deeper W8 taper (halve volume, 'more recovery than a younger program'). Power capped at moderate amplitude with soft landings, peak only 'moderate-to-high' and explicitly amplitude-capped. Mobility/joint prep headlines every week. Minor deduction: power appears (lightly) from W1 rather than being withheld until tissue is prepped, so it is marginally less impact-conservative early than the linear/minimalist models.

Excellent on the lens and the most impact-conservative on paper: power is fully WITHHELD until W5 after four weeks of dedicated tissue/tendon prep, so nothing explosive lands on an under-prepared joint. Explicit 3x/wk, 45-60 min, mobility/recovery bookending every session as headline work. Single modest peak (W7), intensity never maximal, nothing to failure, soft landings only. Genuine W4 deload ('finish feeling you could do more') and true W8 taper (cut ~50%, recovery headlined) are both real, not token. Small deductions: the linear structure means reactive/power qualities are absent for the entire first block, a mild detraining/staleness risk for a player skating through the program (a longevity concern), and it leans slightly more on light DBs/load than the strictly minimalist option.

scores.philosophy: Mobility-forward minimalist for the 40+ competitive rec player
scores.comment: Very strong on the lens: most explicit 'mobility/joint prep is the MAIN EVENT' framing, lowest overall volume, bodyweight/bands/light-DB only, power withheld until W5 and never above moderate amplitude with soft 'stick' landings and low contacts. Peaks deliberately kept low ('a masters peak, not a teenager's'). Genuine W4 deload (~half volume, easy quality) and a deeper-than-younger W8 recovery taper. Best, most consistent plain-language regressions on every hard element ('knees/Achilles cranky? sub low-box step-downs'). Deduction: it never explicitly states the 3x/wk frequency or session count, leaving the weekly structure ambiguous where Designs 1 and 2 nail it; otherwise it would score with the leaders.

scores.philosophy: Concurrent hockey-transfer for the 40-plus masters athlete
scores.comment: Genuinely smart core idea for masters (hold volume flat, ramp INTENSITY not volume; keep all qualities game-ready year-round), and its W8 taper is the best-dosed of the four (cut 50-60%, recovery-weighted). W4 deload is also genuine (40-50%, impact pulled). BUT this is the least conservative on the strict safety lens for two concrete reasons: (1) it introduces moderate-amplitude power in W3, inside the foundation block, rather than withholding it until tissue is prepped as the priority order implies; and (2) it peaks intensity at 'high' across BOTH W6 and W7 — back-to-back high-intensity weeks for a population whose limiter is recovery. 'High' intensity (even at capped amplitude) twice running sails closest to over-dosing the nervous system/tissue and is exactly the pattern the lens warns against. Concurrent everything-every-week also gives the least day-to-day tissue spacing. Strong regressions and 'drop a set on cranky days' language pull it back up, but it lands clearly last on adherence to conservative progression and impact restraint.

scores.bestDesign: Daily-undulating for the 40+ body. On this lens it is the best overall because it doesn't merely assert the 40+ rules, it builds in the mechanism that enforces them: three fixed-flavour sessions (Strength / Speed / Power) recurring every week guarantee that no single vulnerable tissue gets hammered on consecutive days, giving aging connective tissue the 48+ hours it needs to adapt without flaring. It pairs that with an explicit 3x/wk structure, a genuine W4 deload, a real deeper W8 recovery taper, strictly capped moderate-amplitude soft-landing power, no grinding to failure, and mobility/joint prep as the weekly headline. It satisfies every hard rule while also being the most robust against the lens's flagged failure modes.
scores.bestIdeasFromOthers: 1) From Linear durability-first AND Mobility-forward minimalist: WITHHOLD power until Week 5 after a full 4-week tissue/tendon prep block — the daily-undulating model would be even safer if its Power day stayed bodyweight/footwork-only (no jumps) until W5 instead of running at 'easy amplitude' from W1. 2) From Linear durability-first: spell out session length and the explicit 'finish feeling you could do more' deload cue — concrete, self-coachable dosing language. 3) From Mobility-forward minimalist: its consistent, named plain-language regression on EVERY hard element ('cranky knees/Achilles? sub low-box step-downs') is the gold standard for self-coachability and should be grafted onto all designs; also its deliberately low 'masters peak, not a teenager's' framing. 4) From Concurrent hockey-transfer: the 'ramp INTENSITY while holding VOLUME flat' principle (spares tissue, taxes CNS) and the deepest, best-specified W8 taper (cut 50-60%, recovery-weighted) plus the 'drop a set on a cranky day' auto-regulation cue — excellent for keeping a player game-ready through the season. Avoid grafting its back-to-back W6+W7 'high' intensity weeks and its W3 power introduction.
